---
title: "Longitudinal treatments and time-dependent confounding"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{longitudinal-trt}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>"
)
```

```{r setup}
library(ipeval)
library(survival)
```

# Model development

First, simulate model development data. We use scenario 1 (Cox model) as described in Keogh and Van Geloven (2024). Our validation data will be simulated later in the same way as the development data.

```{r}
n <- 3000
n_visits <- 5

gamma_0 <- -1
gamma_L <- 0.5

alpha_0 <- -2
alpha_A <- -0.5
alpha_L <- 0.5
alpha_U <- 0.5

U <- rnorm(n, 0, 0.1)

simulate_longitudinal <- function(n, fix_trt = NULL, seed = 123) {
  set.seed(seed)

  A <- matrix(nrow = n, ncol = n_visits)
  L <- matrix(nrow = n, ncol = n_visits)

  time <- rep(NA, n)
  status <- rep(NA, n)

  simulate_A <- function(i, L, fix_trt) {
    if (is.null(fix_trt)) {
      return(rbinom(n, 1, plogis(gamma_0 + gamma_L * L[, i])))
    } else {
      return(rep(fix_trt, n))
    }
  }

  L[, 1] <- rnorm(n, U, 1)
  A[, 1] <- simulate_A(1, L, fix_trt)

  for (i in 2:n_visits) {
    L[, i] <- rnorm(n, 0.8 * L[, i - 1] - A[, i - 1] + 0.1 * (i-1) + U)
    A[, i] <- ifelse(A[, i - 1] == 1, rep(1, n), simulate_A(i, L, fix_trt))
  }

  for (i in 1:n_visits){
    new.t <- ipeval:::simulate_time_to_event(
      n = n,
      constant_baseline_haz = 1,
      LP = alpha_0 + alpha_A * A[, i] + alpha_L * L[, i] + alpha_U * U
    )
    time <- ifelse(is.na(time) & new.t < 1, i - 1 + new.t,time)
  }
  status <- ifelse(is.na(time), 0, 1)
  time <- ifelse(is.na(time), 5, time)

  colnames(A) <- paste0("A", 0:(n_visits - 1))
  colnames(L) <- paste0("L", 0:(n_visits - 1))

  data.frame(id = 1:n, time, status, A, L, U)
}

df_dev <- simulate_longitudinal(n, seed = 1)

# prepare into long format for fitting Cox model

make_dev_long <- function(df_dev) {
  df_dev_long <- wide_to_long(
    df_dev,
    baseline_variables = c("id", "time", "status", "L0", "U"),
    wide_variables = list(A = c("A0", "A1", "A2", "A3", "A4"),
                          L = c("L0", "L1", "L2", "L3", "L4")),
    visit_times = c(0,1,2,3,4),
    outcome_times = df_dev$time
  )
  
  df_dev_long$time_start <- df_dev_long$visit_time
  df_dev_long$time_end <- pmin(df_dev_long$visit_time + 1, df_dev_long$time)
  df_dev_long$status <- ifelse(
    df_dev_long$time_end == df_dev_long$time, 
    df_dev_long$status, 
    0
  )
  df_dev_long$time <- NULL
  
  df_dev_long <- add_lag_terms(df_dev_long, "A", 1:4)
  df_dev_long
}


df_dev_long <- make_dev_long(df_dev)
head(df_dev)

head(df_dev_long)
```

Now to fit an interventional prediction model capable of estimating risk under always treated strategy and risk under never treated strategy.

```{r}
# TODO: this code needs to be refactored & annotated

fit_long_models <- function(df_dev_long) {
  ipt_visits <- ipeval:::ipt_weights(df_dev_long, A ~ L * A_lag_1)
  
  df_dev_long$iptw_visits <- ipt_visits$weights
  df_dev_long$iptw_cumprod <- unlist(tapply(df_dev_long$iptw_visits,
                                            df_dev_long$id, cumprod))
  
  cox_msm <- coxph(
    Surv(time_start, time_end, status) ~ A + A_lag_1 + A_lag_2 + A_lag_3 + A_lag_4 + L0,
    data = df_dev_long,
    weights = iptw_cumprod
  )
  
  cumhaz <- basehaz(cox_msm, centered = FALSE)$hazard
  event.times <- basehaz(cox_msm, centered = FALSE)$time
  cumhaz.fun <- stepfun(event.times, c(0, cumhaz))
  
  # hazard rate term
  hrt <- function(variable, value) {
    exp(coef(cox_msm)[variable]*value)
  }
  
  # # risk under never treated
  risk0 <- function(t, L0) {
    1 - exp(-cumhaz.fun(t)*hrt("L0", L0))
  }
  
  # # risk under always treated
  risk1 <- function(t, L0) {
    1 - exp(-(
      cumhaz.fun(min(t, 1))*
        hrt("L0", L0)*hrt("A", 1) +
        
        (t >= 1) *
        (cumhaz.fun(min(t, 2)) - cumhaz.fun(1))*
        hrt("L0", L0)*hrt("A", 1)*hrt("A_lag_1", 1) +
        
        (t >= 2) *
        (cumhaz.fun(min(t, 3)) - cumhaz.fun(2))*
        hrt("L0", L0)*hrt("A", 1)*hrt("A_lag_1", 1)*hrt("A_lag_2", 1) +
        
        (t >= 3) *
        (cumhaz.fun(min(t, 4)) - cumhaz.fun(3))*
        hrt("L0", L0)*hrt("A", 1)*hrt("A_lag_1", 1)*hrt("A_lag_2", 1)*hrt("A_lag_3", 1) +
        
        (t >= 4) *
        (cumhaz.fun(min(t, 5)) - cumhaz.fun(4))*
        hrt("L0", L0)*hrt("A", 1)*hrt("A_lag_1", 1)*hrt("A_lag_2", 1)*
        hrt("A_lag_3", 1)*hrt("A_lag_4", 1)
    ))
  }
  return(list(risk0, risk1))
}

models <- fit_long_models(df_dev_long)
risk0 <- models[[1]]
risk1 <- models[[2]]

```

We can use ```risk0(t, L0)``` to estimate the risk under no treatment at time $t$ for a patient with baseline value $L0$. ```risk1(t, L0)``` estimates risk under treatment.

# Validation

This will show the workflow for validating such models when time dependent confounding is an issue. We first simulate a validation data set. We expect the user to start with a dataframe in this (wide) structure. Inputs required for the ip_score_long are:

- a data.frame with the surv(time, status) outcome and id columns
- a data.frame in long format, which will contain the treatment and confounders at each time visit  

The package provides functions to easily generate these data.frames, when starting with data in this wide structure: 

```{r}
df_val <- simulate_longitudinal(n = 3000, seed = 2)
head(df_val)
```

We naturally expect the user to have estimated probabilities corresponding to the intervention(s) of interest.
```{r}
predicted0 <- risk0(5, df_val$L0)
predicted1 <- risk1(5, df_val$L0)
```


First, create the data.frame with the surv status:
```{r}
df_val_outcomes <- df_val[, c("id", "time", "status")]
head(df_val_outcomes)
```

Second, create the data.frame with the time dependent variables. The package provides several convenience functions to help with this. Our treatment model depends on the value of L of the same visit, and previous treatment value.

```{r}
df_val_long <- wide_to_long(
  df_val, 
  baseline_variables = c("id"), 
  wide_variables = list("A" = paste0("A", 0:4), "L" = paste0("L", 0:4)), 
  visit_times = 0:4,
  outcome_times = df_val$time
)

df_val_long <- add_lag_terms(df_val_long, "A")
head(df_val_long)
```

We can then call the validation function:

```{r, fig.width=5, fig.height=5}
ip_score_long(
  probabilities = predicted0, 
  data_outcome = df_val_outcomes, 
  data_long = df_val_long, 
  time_horizon = 5, 
  treatment_formula = A ~ A_lag_1 * L,
  treatment_of_interest = rep(0,5),
  metrics = c("auc", "brier", "scaled_brier", "oeratio", "calplot")
)
```

The assumptions are not yet updated for the longitudinal treatment setting.

The function ```ip_score_long()``` works roughly as follows:

1. It computes the inverse probability of treatment weights at each visit for each patient. 
2. It computes the product of these per patient.
3. We label which patients are faithful to the treatment strategy of interest. Patients are still defined to be faithful when they have the event before being able to complete the entire treatment strategy.
4. We create a 'flat' (1 row per patient) pseudopopulation of all patients that were faithful to the treatment of interest, and use their product iptw as the weight. 
5. We compute the metrics on this pseudopopulation. 

In steps 4 and 5 we simplify the problem by converting the longitudinal treatment strategy into a binary one, which is TRUE if patient is faithful to longitudinal treatment. This allows us to reuse the function logic of the point treatment ```ip_score()```.

Censoring due to non-artifical mechanisms is not implemented yet.  

# Reproducing results Keogh and Van Geloven (2024)

Reproduce results from eTable 7. n = 3000, 1000 simulation runs. Scroll down for the results.

```{r}
metrics <- c("oeratio", "auc", "brier", "scaled_brier")

simulation_run <- function(seed) {
  df_dev <- simulate_longitudinal(n = 3000, seed = seed)
  df_dev_long <- make_dev_long(df_dev)
  
  models <- tryCatch(
      fit_long_models(df_dev_long),
      error = function(e) NULL
    )
  if (is.null(models)) {
    cat("model building failed in seed ", seed, "\n")
    return(NULL)
  }
  risk0 <- models[[1]]
  risk1 <- models[[2]]

  df_val <- simulate_longitudinal(n = 3000, seed = seed + 1)
  predicted0 <- risk0(5, df_val$L0)
  predicted1 <- risk1(5, df_val$L0)

  df_val_outcomes <- df_val[, c("id", "time", "status")]
  df_val_long <- wide_to_long(
    df_val,
    baseline_variables = c("id", "L0"),
    wide_variables = list("A" = paste0("A", 0:4), "L" = paste0("L", 0:4)),
    visit_times = 0:4,
    outcome_times = df_val$time
  )
  df_val_long <- add_lag_terms(df_val_long, "A")

  score0 <- ip_score_long(
    probabilities = predicted0,
    data_outcome = df_val_outcomes,
    data_long = df_val_long,
    time_horizon = 5,
    treatment_formula = A ~ A_lag_1 * L,
    treatment_of_interest = rep(0,5),
    metrics = metrics,
    null_model = FALSE
  )
  score1 <- ip_score_long(
    probabilities = predicted1,
    data_outcome = df_val_outcomes,
    data_long = df_val_long,
    time_horizon = 5,
    treatment_formula = A ~ A_lag_1 * L,
    treatment_of_interest = rep(1,5),
    metrics = metrics,
    null_model = FALSE
  )

  # use same seed as df_val so L0's and predictions coincide. Afterwards
  # treatment is set fixed to never or always to estimate 'truth'.
  df_true0 <- simulate_longitudinal(n = 3000, fix_trt = 0, seed = seed + 1)
  df_true1 <- simulate_longitudinal(n = 3000, fix_trt = 1, seed = seed + 1)

  score0_truth <- observed_score(predicted0, df_true0, status,
                                 metrics = metrics)
  score1_truth <- observed_score(predicted1, df_true1, status, metrics = metrics)


  return(
    list(
      "never-oe-cf" = score0$score$oeratio,
      "never-oe-truth" = score0_truth$score$oeratio,
      "never-oe-bias" = score0$score$oeratio - score0_truth$score$oeratio,

      "never-auc-cf" = score0$score$auc,
      "never-auc-truth" = score0_truth$score$auc,
      "never-auc-bias" = score0$score$auc - score0_truth$score$auc,

      "never-brier-cf" = score0$score$brier,
      "never-brier-truth" = score0_truth$score$brier,
      "never-brier-bias" = score0$score$brier - score0_truth$score$brier,

      "never-scaled_brier-cf" = score0$score$scaled_brier,
      "never-scaled_brier-truth" = score0_truth$score$scaled_brier,
      "never-scaled_brier-bias" = score0$score$scaled_brier - score0_truth$score$scaled_brier,

      "always-oe-cf" = score1$score$oeratio,
      "always-oe-truth" = score1_truth$score$oeratio,
      "always-oe-bias" = score1$score$oeratio - score1_truth$score$oeratio,

      "always-auc-cf" = score1$score$auc,
      "always-auc-truth" = score1_truth$score$auc,
      "always-auc-bias" = score1$score$auc - score1_truth$score$auc,

      "always-brier-cf" = score1$score$brier,
      "always-brier-truth" = score1_truth$score$brier,
      "always-brier-bias" = score1$score$brier - score1_truth$score$brier,

      "always-scaled_brier-cf" = score1$score$scaled_brier,
      "always-scaled_brier-truth" = score1_truth$score$scaled_brier,
      "always-scaled_brier-bias" = score1$score$scaled_brier - score1_truth$score$scaled_brier
    )
  )
}

results <- lapply(1:1000, simulation_run)
results <- Filter(Negate(is.null), results)
```


```{r}
se <- function(x) sd(x, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(sum(!is.na(x)))

summarize <- function(results, key, label, fct = mean) {
  values <- sapply(results, function(x) x[[key]])
  result <- do.call(fct, args = list(values))
  cat(sprintf("%-4s %-5s = %8.3f\n",
              deparse(substitute(fct)),
              label,
              result))
}

for (x in c("never", "always")) {
  for (y in c("oe", "auc", "scaled_brier", "brier")) {

    cat(sprintf("%s treated, %s\n", x, y))
    summarize(results, paste0(x, "-", y, "-truth"), "truth")
    summarize(results, paste0(x, "-", y, "-cf"), "cf")
    summarize(results, paste0(x, "-", y, "-bias"), "bias")
    summarize(results, paste0(x, "-", y, "-bias"), "bias", fct = se)
    cat("\n")
  }
}
```
All estimates look fine and similar to eTable7, except for the scaled Brier score. I do not yet know why this is. 

![eTable 7](etable7.png)
